nativeRange.habitat: Sand and mud flats, estuaries, protected lagoons, and shallow reef margins; typically found on sandy or silty substrates with adjacent rubble or seagrass; depth range 1–55 m

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small live invertebrates — copepods, amphipods, small crustaceans, and zooplankton — which it picks from sandy and rocky substrates. Most specimens have been observed to rarely accept prepared or frozen foods; some individuals may gradually take frozen mysis or enriched brine shrimp with patient training. A well-established refugium and thriving in-tank pod population are generally considered essential for long-term success. Feeding three or more times per day is advisable in systems without sufficient natural prey.

avoidWith: other dragonets (except mated pairs introduced simultaneously)
competitive feeders (dottybacks, damsels, anthias, basslets, gobies)
aggressive species that may outcompete for food

summary: A rare and striking dragonet from the Indo-Pacific known for its elaborate finger-like pelvic fin rays; generally considered suitable for advanced to expert reef keepers who can maintain a steady live prey population.
careNotes:
The Fingered Dragonet (Dactylopus dactylopus) is closely related to the Green Mandarin but tends to be larger, rarer in the trade, and arguably even more demanding to keep long-term. Like other Callionymidae, it has been documented grazing continuously on small live invertebrates from sandy and rocky substrates. Wild-caught specimens have generally been observed to refuse prepared or frozen foods; patience and a mature refugium with strong copepod and amphipod populations are widely considered the most important factors for success. Tanks are generally advised to be at least 6–12 months old before introduction.

Only one male has been reported to be maintainable per display tank; males tend to be aggressive toward each other but otherwise are generally peaceful with dissimilar species. A single male may be kept with one or more females; multiple specimens have been reported to coexist if introduced simultaneously.

The species avoids the open water column in favor of walking along the substrate using its modified pelvic fins — a highly distinctive behavior that sets it apart from most other marine fish. It is generally considered reef safe and has not been widely reported to damage corals or motile invertebrates.

Care level disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria rates this species as "difficult" (mapped to advanced); BlueZoo Aquatics rates it "expert-only." The value "advanced" reflects an average between these two assessments. For keepers new to dragonets, expert-level preparation is advisable given the feeding challenges.

Temperature cross-source note: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild-habitat data gives 24.5–29°C. The averaged range of 23–27°C is used here; values toward the upper FishBase range may be acceptable for short periods but are not the target recommended for aquariums.

Minimum tank volume: sources disagree significantly — web search references cite 50 US gallons (~189 L), LiveAquaria cites 70 gallons (~265 L), BlueZoo cites 75 gallons (~284 L), and ReefApp recommends 500 L (~132 gal). The averaged value of approximately 310 L is used. The wide spread (nearly 3×) is flagged for reviewer attention; the practical driver is prey availability, suggesting the larger end of recommendations is appropriate for long-term maintenance.

Note on size: FishBase reports a maximum of 30 cm TL but notes a typical mature standard length of ~10 cm. Aquarium specimens are typically sold at 4–13 cm and may grow considerably larger than the closely related Mandarin Dragonet. The range 10–30 cm reflects available data.

Copper sensitivity: as a scaleless, mucus-covered dragonet, this species should be assumed highly sensitive to copper-based medications, consistent with other Callionymidae (no typed field exists for fish copper sensitivity; see field-gap suggestion).
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Dactylopus dactylopus has not been well documented in the aquarium hobby; successful spawning and larval rearing appear to be exceptionally rare. Captive pairs have been reported to occasionally spawn in the evening in a behavior similar to other dragonets (ascending together toward the water surface), but larval rearing is considered highly advanced and has rarely if ever been reported to full juvenile stage outside of specialist research settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species-specific product page confirmed: scientific name, common names (Fingered Dragonet, Mother Sailfin Dragonet), care level (difficult), temperament (peaceful), reef compatibility (yes), adult size (11 in / ~28 cm), temperature (72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), minimum tank (70 gal / ~265 L), diet (carnivore, live foods), and native range (Indo-Pacific). The collection page https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/dragonets was checked first but lacked species-specific numeric data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified: scientific name, taxonomy (Callionymidae / Callionymiformes), maximum size (30.0 cm TL; typical 10 cm SL), native range (Western Pacific: Japan to Australia, including Guam and Palau), temperature range (24.5–29°C), habitat (sandy/muddy substrates, estuaries, 1–55 m depth). FishBase did not specify pH, salinity, or tank size. Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (FishBase upper bound 29°C vs LiveAquaria 78°F / 25.6°C); values averaged.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-verified: adult size (~12 in / ~30.5 cm), minimum tank (75 gal / ~284 L), care level (expert-only), temperament (peaceful), reef safe (yes), native range (Indo-Pacific), diet (live copepods and amphipods, rarely frozen). Tank must be at least 6–8 months old. Care level 'expert-only' is more conservative than LiveAquaria's 'difficult'; 'advanced' used as average.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: adult size 30 cm, minimum tank 500 L, temperature ~26°C, salinity ~1.026, diet (small crustaceans and zooplankton), temperament (mostly peaceful, may be aggressive toward similar species). The 500 L tank recommendation is notably higher than other sources; all four tank-size values averaged to ~310 L. The wide spread (189–500 L, ~3×) is flagged.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning events have occasionally been reported but successful larval rearing to juvenile stage appears to be extremely rare if documented at all; considered not viable in captivity under normal hobbyist conditions.
